title: The Revolt of 1857 — First War of Independence
intro: Let's learn about the Revolt of 1857 — the first large-scale, organised challenge to British rule, called by V.D. Savarkar the 'First War of Independence'.
explanation:
- **Causes**: political (Doctrine of Lapse — Satara, Jhansi, Nagpur annexed; Awadh annexed 1856 on 'misgovernance'), economic (ruin of peasants and artisans), social-religious interference, and military grievances.
- **Immediate cause**: the **greased cartridges** of the new Enfield rifle (cow and pig fat) offended both Hindu and Muslim sepoys.
- **Beginning**: **Mangal Pandey** at Barrackpore (29 March 1857); the revolt proper began at **Meerut on 10 May 1857**; sepoys marched to Delhi and proclaimed **Bahadur Shah Zafar** emperor.
- **Leaders/centres**: Delhi — Bakht Khan; Kanpur — Nana Saheb, Tantia Tope; Jhansi — **Rani Lakshmibai**; Lucknow — Begum Hazrat Mahal; Bihar — **Kunwar Singh**; Faizabad — Maulavi Ahmadullah.
- **Results**: **Government of India Act 1858** — Company rule ended, Crown took over; Queen Victoria's Proclamation; army reorganised; Doctrine of Lapse abandoned.
- **In Hyderabad State**: **Turrebaz Khan and Maulvi Allauddin** attacked the British Residency (17 July 1857) — Telangana's own 1857 heroes!
example: **Turrebaz Khan's attack on the Hyderabad Residency** shows TGPSC's pattern of linking national events to Telangana — remember him and Maulvi Allauddin whenever 1857 is asked.

test.q: The Revolt of 1857 began at Meerut on:
test.why: Sepoys at Meerut mutinied on 10 May 1857 and marched to Delhi.

test.q: Who was proclaimed Emperor of India by the rebels?
test.why: The aged Mughal Bahadur Shah Zafar became the symbolic head.

test.q: Who led the 1857 attack on the British Residency in Hyderabad?
test.why: They attacked the Residency on 17 July 1857; Turrebaz Khan was later shot.

test.q: Which Act transferred power from the Company to the British Crown?
test.why: The 1858 Act ended Company rule after the revolt.

test.q: The Doctrine of Lapse is associated with:
test.why: Dalhousie used it to annex states like Satara, Jhansi and Nagpur.

title: Formation of the Indian National Congress and the Moderates
intro: Let's learn how the Indian National Congress was born in 1885 and how its first generation — the Moderates — worked.
explanation:
- **Foundation**: INC founded on **28 December 1885** at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College, **Bombay**; 72 delegates; first president **W.C. Bonnerjee**; retired ICS officer **A.O. Hume** was the chief organiser ('safety valve' theory is debated).
- **Moderate era (1885–1905)**: leaders — Dadabhai Naoroji, Pherozeshah Mehta, Surendranath Banerjea, G.K. Gokhale (Gandhi's political guru).
- **Methods**: petitions, prayers, resolutions, articles — the '3 Ps'; faith in British justice; demands for Indianisation of services, council reforms, cut in military spending.
- **Big contribution**: **economic critique of colonialism** — Dadabhai Naoroji's **Drain Theory** ('Poverty and Un-British Rule in India'); R.C. Dutt's 'Economic History of India'.
- **Achievements**: Indian Councils Act 1892; creation of national political consciousness. **Limitation**: narrow social base, no mass involvement.
example: **Dadabhai Naoroji's Drain Theory** — that India's wealth was being 'drained' to Britain through home charges, remittances and trade — gave the movement its economic backbone; he was also the first Indian elected to the British House of Commons.

test.q: The first session of the INC (1885) was held at:
test.why: It met at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College, Bombay, with 72 delegates.

test.q: The first president of the INC was:
test.why: W.C. Bonnerjee presided; Hume was the organiser, not president.

test.q: The 'Drain of Wealth' theory was propounded by:
test.why: Naoroji explained it in 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India'.

test.q: Gandhi regarded whom as his political guru?
test.why: Gandhi called Gokhale his political guru and mentor.

test.q: The Moderates' methods are best described as:
options: Petitions, prayers and protests within law
test.why: They believed in constitutional agitation and faith in British justice.

title: Partition of Bengal, Swadeshi Movement and the Surat Split
intro: Let's learn about Curzon's 1905 partition of Bengal and the storm it unleashed — Swadeshi, Boycott, and the Congress split of 1907.
explanation:
- **Partition (16 October 1905)**: Curzon divided Bengal citing administration; real aim — divide Hindus and Muslims and weaken nationalism.
- **Swadeshi & Boycott**: use Indian goods, boycott British goods; bonfires of foreign cloth; national education (**Bengal National College**, Aurobindo as principal); Vande Mataram became the rallying song; Rabindranath Tagore's **Rakhi Bandhan** united communities.
- **Extremist leaders**: **Lal-Bal-Pal** (Lala Lajpat Rai, Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Bipin Chandra Pal) and Aurobindo demanded **Swaraj**; Tilak: 'Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it.'
- **Surat Split (1907)**: Congress split into Moderates and Extremists over methods and the presidency.
- **Muslim League founded (Dhaka, 1906)**; **Morley-Minto Reforms (1909)** introduced **separate electorates** for Muslims.
- **Result**: partition **annulled in 1911**; capital shifted from Calcutta to **Delhi (1911)**.
example: The **boycott of Manchester cloth** hit British trade measurably and gave India its first taste of economic mass struggle — the same technique Gandhi would later scale up nationwide.

test.q: Bengal was partitioned in 1905 by:
test.why: Curzon carried out the partition, effective 16 October 1905.

test.q: Lal-Bal-Pal refers to:
options: Lajpat Rai, Tilak and Bipin Chandra Pal
test.why: The famous Extremist trio led the Swadeshi upsurge.

test.q: The Congress split between Moderates and Extremists happened at:
test.why: The Surat session of 1907 ended in an open split.

test.q: Separate electorates for Muslims were introduced by:
test.why: The 1909 reforms created communal electorates — a seed of partition.

test.q: The Partition of Bengal was annulled in:
test.why: It was annulled in 1911, when the capital also moved to Delhi.

title: Gandhi's Arrival and Early Satyagrahas (1915–1919)
intro: Let's learn how Gandhi, returning from South Africa in 1915, tested satyagraha at Champaran, Kheda and Ahmedabad — and how Jallianwala Bagh changed everything.
explanation:
- **Return**: Gandhi reached India on **9 January 1915** (now Pravasi Bharatiya Divas); toured India for a year on Gokhale's advice; founded **Sabarmati Ashram**.
- **Champaran Satyagraha (1917)** — first civil disobedience in India: against the **tinkathia system** forcing indigo cultivation in Bihar; Gandhi defied orders to leave; planters' exploitation ended. Rajendra Prasad and J.B. Kripalani assisted.
- **Kheda Satyagraha (1918)** — first non-cooperation: crop failure in Gujarat, government refused tax remission; peasants withheld revenue; Sardar **Vallabhbhai Patel** emerged.
- **Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918)** — first hunger strike: for mill workers' 35% wage hike — successful.
- **Rowlatt Act (1919)**: detention without trial — 'no vakil, no appeal, no dalil'; nationwide **Rowlatt Satyagraha**, Gandhi's first all-India movement.
- **Jallianwala Bagh (13 April 1919)**: General **Dyer** fired on a peaceful Baisakhi gathering at Amritsar; hundreds killed; Tagore renounced his knighthood; **Hunter Commission** inquiry followed.
example: At **Champaran**, Gandhi turned a local indigo grievance into a moral confrontation the Raj could not handle — the model (local issue → mass mobilisation → negotiated victory) he reused across the freedom struggle.

test.q: Gandhi's first civil disobedience experiment in India was at:
test.why: Champaran (1917) against the tinkathia indigo system.

test.q: The 'tinkathia' system compelled peasants to:
options: Grow indigo on 3/20th of their land
test.why: Tenants had to plant indigo on 3 kathas per bigha for planters.

test.q: Sardar Patel first rose to prominence during:
test.why: He led the no-revenue campaign in Kheda (1918).

test.q: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre took place on:
test.why: Dyer's troops fired on the Baisakhi-day gathering, 13 April 1919.

test.q: Who renounced his knighthood in protest against Jallianwala Bagh?
test.why: Tagore returned his knighthood in protest.

title: Non-Cooperation and Khilafat Movement (1920–22)
intro: Let's learn about Gandhi's first nationwide mass movement — Non-Cooperation — and why he suddenly withdrew it after Chauri Chaura.
explanation:
- **Khilafat issue**: Muslims angered by harsh treatment of the Turkish Caliph after WWI; **Ali brothers** (Muhammad Ali & Shaukat Ali) led the Khilafat Committee; Gandhi saw a chance for **Hindu-Muslim unity**.
- **Launch**: Congress's **Nagpur session (1920)** approved Non-Cooperation; goal — **Swaraj within a year**.
- **Programme**: boycott of government schools, colleges, courts, councils, foreign cloth; surrender of titles (Gandhi returned Kaiser-i-Hind); national schools (Jamia Millia, Kashi Vidyapith); **charkha** and khadi promoted.
- **Spread**: students left colleges, lawyers like **Motilal Nehru and C.R. Das** gave up practice; no-tax campaigns.
- **Chauri Chaura (5 February 1922)**: a mob burnt a police station in Gorakhpur district, UP, killing 22 policemen; Gandhi, committed to non-violence, **withdrew the movement** (12 February 1922) — many leaders were dismayed.
- **Aftermath**: Gandhi jailed; **Swarajists** (C.R. Das, Motilal Nehru) chose council entry; movement's legacy — Congress became a true mass organisation.
example: Gandhi calling off a movement at its peak over **22 deaths at Chauri Chaura** is the classic example of means mattering more than ends in Gandhian politics — a favourite analytical question.

test.q: The Non-Cooperation Movement was approved at which Congress session?
test.why: The Nagpur session (December 1920) endorsed the programme.

test.q: The Khilafat Movement was led by:
test.why: Muhammad Ali and Shaukat Ali led the Khilafat agitation.

test.q: The Chauri Chaura incident occurred on:
test.why: The police-station burning on 5 Feb 1922 led Gandhi to halt the movement.

test.q: After withdrawal of Non-Cooperation, the Swarajya Party was formed by:
test.why: They advocated entering legislative councils to obstruct from within.

test.q: Which title did Gandhi return during Non-Cooperation?
test.why: Gandhi surrendered his Kaiser-i-Hind medal awarded for war services.

title: Civil Disobedience — Dandi March and the Round Tables (1928–34)
intro: Let's learn about the Simon boycott, Purna Swaraj, the Salt March of 1930, and the Gandhi-Irwin pact.
explanation:
- **Simon Commission (1928)**: all-white commission — 'Simon Go Back'; lathi-charge injuries killed **Lala Lajpat Rai** (Lahore).
- **Nehru Report (1928)** demanded dominion status; Jinnah replied with his **Fourteen Points**.
- **Lahore Session (December 1929)**: Jawaharlal Nehru president; **Purna Swaraj** resolution; **26 January 1930** celebrated as first Independence Day (later chosen as Republic Day).
- **Dandi March (12 March – 6 April 1930)**: Gandhi with **78 followers** walked **~240 miles** from Sabarmati to Dandi and broke the **salt law**. Across India: C. Rajagopalachari (Vedaranyam march), K. Kelappan (Malabar); **Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan's Khudai Khidmatgars** in NWFP; Rani Gaidinliu in the Northeast.
- **First Round Table Conference (1930)** — Congress absent; **Gandhi-Irwin Pact (March 1931)** — movement suspended, Gandhi attended the **Second RTC (1931)** which failed on communal issues.
- **Communal Award (1932)** gave separate electorates to Dalits — Gandhi's fast → **Poona Pact** with **Dr. B.R. Ambedkar** (reserved seats in joint electorates). Movement withdrawn 1934.
example: **Salt** was chosen because every Indian, rich or poor, used it — taxing it symbolised colonial exploitation of the humblest household; a perfect example of Gandhi converting an everyday item into a national weapon.

test.q: The Purna Swaraj resolution was adopted at which session?
test.why: The Lahore session under Nehru declared complete independence the goal.

test.q: The Dandi March began on:
test.why: Gandhi left Sabarmati on 12 March and broke the salt law on 6 April 1930.

test.q: The Khudai Khidmatgars were led by:
test.why: The 'Frontier Gandhi' led the Red Shirts in NWFP.

test.q: The Poona Pact (1932) was signed between Gandhi and:
test.why: It replaced separate electorates for Dalits with reserved seats.

test.q: Which leader died after lathi blows during the anti-Simon protests?
test.why: Lajpat Rai was injured at Lahore in 1928 and died soon after.

title: Quit India Movement and the INA (1942–45)
intro: Let's learn about the final mass upsurge — Quit India, 1942 — and Subhas Chandra Bose's Indian National Army.
explanation:
- **Background**: failure of the **Cripps Mission (1942)** ('a post-dated cheque'); WWII threats; rising prices.
- **Launch**: Congress met at **Gowalia Tank, Bombay, 8 August 1942**; Gandhi's mantra — **'Do or Die'**. Leaders arrested overnight (Operation Zero Hour), so the movement went leaderless and spontaneous.
- **Underground/parallel activity**: **Jayaprakash Narayan, Aruna Asaf Ali** (hoisted the flag at Gowalia Tank), **Usha Mehta's secret Congress Radio**; parallel governments at **Ballia (UP), Tamluk (Bengal), Satara (Maharashtra)**.
- **INA**: Bose escaped house arrest (1941), reached Germany then Japan; took over the **Indian National Army** (founded from POWs by Mohan Singh, revived by Bose 1943); formed the **Azad Hind Government** in Singapore (21 October 1943); slogans — **'Jai Hind'**, **'Give me blood, I will give you freedom'**; INA fought at **Imphal-Kohima**; the **Red Fort INA trials (1945)** of Shah Nawaz, Sahgal and Dhillon triggered nationwide sympathy — even the **Royal Indian Navy revolt (1946)**.
example: The **INA trials** backfired on the British: defending officers of every faith together, the nation saw the army itself turning nationalist — the RIN Mutiny of 1946 convinced Britain its Indian empire was no longer holdable.

test.q: The Quit India resolution was passed on 8 August 1942 at:
test.why: The AICC met at Gowalia Tank Maidan (August Kranti Maidan).

test.q: Gandhi's slogan for Quit India was:
test.why: 'Karo ya Maro' — Do or Die — was the Quit India mantra.

test.q: The secret Congress Radio during Quit India was run by:
test.why: Usha Mehta broadcast the underground Congress Radio from Bombay.

test.q: The Azad Hind Government was proclaimed by Bose at Singapore in:
test.why: Bose announced the Provisional Government of Free India on 21 October 1943.

test.q: The INA 'Red Fort trials' involved which officers?
test.why: The joint trial of the three officers in 1945 stirred national outrage.

title: Towards Freedom — Cabinet Mission to Independence (1946–47)
intro: Let's learn the final lap: Cabinet Mission, Mountbatten Plan, the Independence Act — and the price of Partition.
explanation:
- **Cabinet Mission (1946)**: three-member team (Pethick-Lawrence, Cripps, Alexander); rejected Pakistan; proposed a **three-tier grouping plan** and a **Constituent Assembly** (elected November 1946, first met **9 December 1946**).
- **Direct Action Day (16 August 1946)**: League's call led to the Great Calcutta Killings; communal violence spread (Noakhali — Gandhi's peace march).
- **Interim Government (September 1946)**: Nehru as Vice-President of the Executive Council; League joined later but obstructed.
- **Mountbatten Plan (3 June 1947)**: partition accepted; provinces to choose; **Radcliffe Commission** drew the boundaries.
- **Indian Independence Act (July 1947)**: two dominions — India (15 August 1947) and Pakistan (14 August); princely states freed to accede (Sardar Patel + V.P. Menon integrated 560+ states; Junagadh, Kashmir and **Hyderabad** were the difficult three — Hyderabad joined after **Operation Polo, September 1948**).
- Gandhi spent Independence Day in **Calcutta**, fasting for communal peace, not celebrating.
example: **Hyderabad's accession (17 September 1948)** connects the national story to Telangana: while the tricolour rose in Delhi in 1947, Telangana had to wait 13 more months for freedom from the Nizam — a comparison TGPSC frequently asks.

test.q: The Constituent Assembly first met on:
test.why: Its first sitting was 9 December 1946 with Sachchidananda Sinha as interim president.

test.q: Direct Action Day (16 August 1946) was called by:
test.why: Jinnah's League called it, leading to the Calcutta killings.

test.q: The boundary lines of partition were drawn by:
test.why: The Radcliffe Commission demarcated Punjab and Bengal borders.

test.q: Who is credited, with V.P. Menon, for integrating the princely states?
test.why: Patel's statesmanship brought 560+ states into the Union.

test.q: Which princely state joined India only after Operation Polo in 1948?
test.why: Hyderabad acceded after the police action of 13–17 September 1948.
